text:
MULTIDISCIPLINARY ONCOLOGY OFFICE VISIT NOTE
Genitourinary Medical Oncology

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with known metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for oncologist office assessment following multidisciplinary tumor board (MTB) review of his case. He was referred to MTB given confirmed visceral disease progression on olaparib, newly identified hepatic and pulmonary metastases, histologic evidence of early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine differentiation, and a rising PSA. The purpose of today's visit is to discuss MTB recommendations, review all available data, and determine a next line of treatment in the setting of visceral crisis and evolving disease biology.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**
Mr. Kowalski is well known to this service. Briefly, he was diagnosed approximately 3 years ago with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), presenting with a PSA of 187 ng/mL, Gleason 9 (4+5) disease, bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, a right obturator lymph node metastasis, and bone metastases to L3-L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck. Germline testing at diagnosis revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), consistent with a hereditary cancer predisposition given his family history (brother with pancreatic cancer, sister with BRCA2-associated breast cancer). Somatic NGS at that time showed biallelic BRCA2 inactivation (VAF 78%), TP53 p.R248W, PTEN homozygous deletion, and RB1 heterozygous deletion — a highly unfavorable genomic profile.

He was initially treated with ADT (leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM q3 months) plus abiraterone/prednisone (Zytiga 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID) per LATITUDE/STAMPEDE intensification data. He had an excellent initial response, with PSA nadiring at 0.8 ng/mL, before developing biochemical and radiographic castration resistance at roughly 24 months — meeting PCWG3 biochemical progression criteria, with a rising PSA from 0.8 to 4.7 ng/mL and new PSMA-avid bone lesions on Ga-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT (new lesions at T10, right posterior 7th rib, left posterior 9th rib, and right sacral ala).

He then received enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily, again with a favorable initial biochemical response (PSA nadir 0.6 ng/mL), before progressing at approximately 6 months with a rising PSA (8.3 ng/mL) and extensive new osseous disease on repeat PSMA PET/CT — new lesions at bilateral iliac crests, L1-L2, sternum, multiple new rib lesions, and enlargement of the right obturator node to 2.1 cm. A CT-guided bone biopsy of a right iliac crest lesion at that time confirmed metastatic Gleason GG5 adenocarcinoma without neuroendocrine transdifferentiation, with AR positivity and BRCA2 homozygous deletion by FISH.

Given his biallelic BRCA2 loss, olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg PO BID was initiated as the third line of therapy, consistent with PROfound trial data. He demonstrated an initial PSA response (nadir 2.1 ng/mL), but his course was complicated by recurrent Grade 3 anemia requiring two separate pRBC transfusions (first at 3 months, second at 6 months), necessitating a dose reduction to 200 mg BID. Despite the dose reduction, he continued to have PSA response (2.8 ng/mL at 4 months) and imaging showed partial response at 5 months, with the right obturator node shrinking to 1.2 cm and improving bone scan signal at multiple sites.

However, approximately 8 months after starting olaparib, he developed a rapidly rising PSA (from nadir 2.1 to 14.6 ng/mL), new right upper quadrant discomfort, and a 6-lb unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks. Physical examination at that visit noted mild hepatomegaly. Urgent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast demonstrated visceral disease spread: new hepatic metastases in the right (3 lesions, largest 2.8 cm) and left (1 lesion, 1.4 cm) hepatic lobes, new pulmonary nodules in the right lower lobe (0.9 cm) and left upper lobe (0.7 cm), enlargement of the right obturator lymph node (3.1 cm), new left external iliac lymph node (1.8 cm), and progressive osseous disease. A CT-guided liver biopsy was performed and pathology revealed metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with focal neuroendocrine differentiation (synaptophysin positive in 10-15% of cells, chromogranin A focally positive), a Ki-67 proliferation index of 55%, and retained AR expression (moderate, 2+) — histologic findings consistent with early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC). Repeat somatic NGS on the liver biopsy showed AR amplification (copy number 12, newly acquired), an AR ligand-binding domain mutation (p.L702H, VAF 22%), newly acquired RB1 homozygous deletion, MYC amplification (copy number 18, increased from copy number 6 at diagnosis), PIK3CA p.H1047R (VAF 18%, newly detected), and no BRCA2 reversion mutations — indicating both AR pathway reactivation and frank clonal evolution. BRCA2 frameshift was retained.

The case was presented to the GU multidisciplinary tumor board, which convened this week. MTB attendees included GU medical oncology, radiation oncology, urology, pathology, radiology, palliative care, and genomics. MTB recommendation: systemic platinum-based chemotherapy with carboplatin/etoposide was felt to be the most appropriate next step given the histologic evidence of neuroendocrine differentiation, aggressive disease biology, and visceral spread. Cabazitaxel was discussed as an alternative for the pure adenocarcinoma component but felt to be less optimal given the NE features and rapid visceral progression. Clinical trial enrollment was explored but no currently available trials matched his molecular profile and status at this center.

Mr. Kowalski is accompanied today by his daughter. He reports continued right upper quadrant discomfort, moderate fatigue, and decreased appetite. He denies acute confusion, fevers, chills, or lower extremity swelling.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**
Constitutional: Fatigue (moderate), unintentional weight loss (~6 lbs over 6 weeks), decreased appetite.
Neurological: Mild cognitive slowing (chronic, attributed to ADT and enzalutamide exposure, improved somewhat since enzalutamide discontinuation). No new focal neurological deficits, no headaches, no visual changes, no bowel or bladder dysfunction.
Respiratory: Mild dyspnea on moderate exertion (improved since last transfusion). No hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Right upper quadrant discomfort, intermittent nausea (Grade 1). No vomiting, melena, or hematochezia. No jaundice reported.
Musculoskeletal: Low back pain (L3-L4 region, Grade 1-2, managed with scheduled acetaminophen and PRN low-dose oxycodone). New right hip discomfort (Grade 1), manageable.
Endocrine/metabolic: Hot flashes (chronic, ADT-related). No peripheral edema.
Hematologic: Fatigue as above; previously required transfusions; currently stable without acute symptomatic anemia.

---

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY**
(Documented at initial GU oncology consultation; reviewed and updated)
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) — as detailed above
2. Hypertension — managed with amlodipine 10 mg daily; required adjustment at initiation of abiraterone
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled on metformin 1000 mg BID; last HbA1c 6.8% (3 months ago)
4. Hyperlipidemia — on atorvastatin 40 mg daily
5. Recurrent olaparib-related myelosuppression requiring pRBC transfusions (x2) and olaparib dose reduction
6. Prior Grade 2 fatigue and Grade 1 fall on enzalutamide (physical therapy completed)

**FAMILY HISTORY**
Brother — pancreatic cancer, deceased in 60s
Sister — breast cancer (BRCA2-associated), treated and alive
Patient carries germline pathogenic BRCA2 variant; appropriate cascade genetic counseling previously arranged for first-degree relatives

**SOCIAL HISTORY**
Retired steelworker. Married, lives with wife. Daughter actively involved in care. Non-smoker (lifetime). No significant alcohol use. No illicit drug use. Functional prior to illness; now with progressive functional decline over recent months given disease burden. Resides in area with reliable transportation to infusion suite.

**ALLERGIES**
NKDA (no known drug allergies)

**MEDICATIONS (as of this visit)**
1. Leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (ongoing ADT)
2. Olaparib (Lynparza) 200 mg PO BID — being discontinued today
3. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SC every 4 weeks (bone protection)
4. Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily
5. Metformin 1000 mg PO BID
6. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily
7. Calcium carbonate 500 mg PO BID + vitamin D3 1000 IU PO daily (bone health supplementation)
8. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h scheduled (pain management)
9. Oxycodone 5 mg PO q6h PRN (breakthrough pain)
10. Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN (nausea)

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION**
General: Appears fatigued, mildly uncomfortable; alert and oriented x3; cooperative. Noticeably thinner compared with prior visits.
Vitals: Blood pressure 132/80 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 98.2°F, oxygen saturation 96% on room air, weight 168 lbs (down from 174 lbs at last visit 4 weeks ago).
ECOG Performance Status: 2-3 (capable of self-care, not confined to bed, but in bed or chair more than 50% of waking hours).
Head/Eyes/ENT: Sclera anicteric. Mucous membranes moist. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains.
Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No wheezing or rales.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1, S2 normal. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation. Liver edge palpable approximately 4 cm below the right costal margin, consistent with hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No ascites on examination.
Extremities: No significant peripheral edema. Right hip with mild tenderness on internal rotation; no obvious deformity.
Neurological: Alert, oriented. Cranial nerves intact. Motor strength 4+/5 diffusely in lower extremities (generalized deconditioning). Gait slow but stable with use of cane. No focal neurological deficits.
Skin: Pallor consistent with anemia. No jaundice or icterus.
Musculoskeletal: Midline lumbar tenderness to deep palpation at L3-L4 level. No point tenderness along vertebral column above T10.

---

**LABORATORY RESULTS**
PSA: 18.4 ng/mL (up from 14.6 ng/mL at last visit; prior nadir on olaparib was 2.1 ng/mL)
Testosterone: < 20 ng/dL (castrate)
Chromogranin A: 142 ng/mL (elevated; upper limit of normal < 100 ng/mL) — consistent with neuroendocrine disease activity
Neuron-specific enolase (NSE): 28 U/L (elevated; upper limit of normal approximately 17 U/L)
LDH: 268 U/L (mildly elevated)

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
  - AST: 52 U/L (mildly elevated; ULN 40)
  - ALT: 48 U/L (mildly elevated; ULN 40)
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 198 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone and hepatic metastases)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL (normal)
  - Albumin: 3.1 g/dL (low-normal, reflects nutritional decline)
  - Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (stable; eGFR 64 mL/min/1.73m2)
  - Glucose: 112 mg/dL (fasting, well-controlled)
  - Sodium/potassium/chloride/bicarbonate: within normal limits

CBC:
  - Hemoglobin: 8.9 g/dL (Grade 2-3 anemia; slightly improved from last documented 7.6 g/dL post-transfusion)
  - Hematocrit: 27.1%
  - Platelets: 148 K/uL (low-normal)
  - WBC: 4.1 K/uL (low-normal, recovering from prior myelosuppression)
  - ANC: 2,800/uL (adequate)

Coagulation: PT 12.8 sec, INR 1.1 (normal)

---

**IMAGING RESULTS**
Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (approximately 1 week ago): As reviewed above and consistent with hepatic metastases (right lobe, largest 2.8 cm; plus left lobe lesion 1.4 cm), bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastases (right lower lobe 0.9 cm, left upper lobe 0.7 cm), right obturator lymph node now 3.1 cm (markedly enlarged from 1.2 cm on prior olaparib response scan), new left external iliac lymph node 1.8 cm, and diffuse progression of osseous metastases. The liver findings and degree of nodal progression are particularly concerning.

The pulmonary nodules are new from the prior restaging scan at 5 months on olaparib, which showed no pulmonary lesions. The liver lesions are likewise new, consistent with visceral spread in the context of olaparib resistance.

---

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

*Problem 1: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with visceral progression on olaparib, treatment-emergent neuroendocrine features*

Mr. Kowalski has confirmed disease progression on olaparib 200 mg BID, with visceral spread (liver, lung), extensive bone and nodal disease progression, an elevated and rising PSA (18.4 ng/mL), and histologic evidence of early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC) based on liver biopsy results showing focal synaptophysin and chromogranin A positivity in 10-15% of tumor cells, with a Ki-67 of 55%. This is supported by elevated circulating neuroendocrine markers (CgA 142 ng/mL, NSE 28 U/L). Somatic molecular profiling from the liver biopsy is additionally alarming, showing newly acquired AR amplification (copy number 12), AR LBD p.L702H mutation (conferring resistance to second-generation androgen receptor pathway inhibitors), newly acquired RB1 homozygous deletion (a key facilitator of NE transdifferentiation, consistent with histology), MYC amplification (copy number 18), and a new PIK3CA p.H1047R mutation. No BRCA2 reversion mutations were identified, meaning olaparib resistance appears to operate through bypass mechanisms rather than restored HR repair.

The GU multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed this case and recommended carboplatin/etoposide chemotherapy as the most appropriate next systemic option, given:
  - Histologic evidence of NE transdifferentiation (t-NEPC), which has well-documented sensitivity to platinum-based regimens (carboplatin + etoposide)
  - Prior exposure to both abiraterone and enzalutamide (AR pathway exhausted)
  - BRCA2 status: olaparib resistance established; no reversion mutations; platinum-based therapy has additional mechanistic rationale in BRCA2-deficient tumors given its role in inducing double-strand DNA breaks that HRR-deficient cells cannot repair
  - Visceral disease burden (hepatic and pulmonary) requiring a response-oriented systemic approach
  - Cabazitaxel discussed but felt to be less appropriate as frontline in the NE-predominant setting, though could be considered in the second-line setting
  - Clinical trial options reviewed; no currently open matching trials at this institution

Plan: Initiate carboplatin AUC 5 IV on Day 1, plus etoposide 100 mg/m2 IV on Days 1, 2, and 3, every 21 days. Cycle 1 Day 1 to be scheduled at the infusion suite pending completion of pre-chemotherapy requirements.
  - Pre-chemotherapy labs: repeat CBC with differential, CMP, and ECG to confirm adequate baseline
  - Supportive G-CSF (filgrastim or pegfilgrastim) will be planned prophylactically given prior myelosuppression history, low baseline hemoglobin, borderline WBC, and anticipated bone marrow compromise from disease and prior PARP inhibitor therapy
  - Anti-emetics: Ondansetron + dexamethasone pre-treatment; prochlorperazine PRN; aprepitant (Emend) to be added given anticipated emetogenic potential of the carboplatin/etoposide combination
  - Continue ADT (leuprolide) — ongoing castrate state to be maintained throughout chemotherapy; next leuprolide injection due in 6 weeks
  - Continue denosumab (Xgeva) for bone protection — reinforce dental evaluation (last dental exam on file 9 months ago; will confirm prior to next dose; jaw osteonecrosis risk counseling provided)
  - Olaparib (Lynparza) DISCONTINUED today

*Problem 2: Anemia (chronic, multifactorial — myelosuppression from olaparib, bone marrow metastatic infiltration, underlying iron deficiency)*

Hgb 8.9 g/dL, symptomatic but currently not meeting threshold for immediate transfusion. Prior transfusions x2 for Grade 3 anemia. If Hgb drops below 8 g/dL or patient becomes more symptomatic, will transfuse again. Ferritin and iron studies to be rechecked at next visit. IV iron replacement considered if iron deficient at that time. Hematology continues to co-manage. Note: ESA therapy remains contraindicated given active malignancy context and thromboembolic risk.

*Problem 3: Goals of care and palliative support*

Extensive and substantive discussion held today with Mr. Kowalski and his daughter regarding the trajectory of his illness. I explained in direct but compassionate terms that his cancer has developed new spread to the liver and lungs, has features of a more aggressive subtype (neuroendocrine), and has exhausted several prior therapies. I also explained that carboplatin/etoposide is the recommended next treatment, with the dual goal of controlling the NE component and managing his visceral disease burden. I was honest that this remains a palliative treatment approach.

Mr. Kowalski articulated a clear understanding of the palliative intent of further therapy. He expressed that he wishes to pursue one additional line of treatment while maintaining quality of life, and specifically deferred hospice enrollment at this time. His goals are to maintain independence for as long as possible and to avoid prolonged hospitalization. He confirmed full code status at this time but asked that we revisit code status discussions at a future visit as the clinical situation evolves.

Palliative care is formally integrated into the treatment plan — palliative care attending was present at the tumor board and will be co-managing Mr. Kowalski throughout the chemotherapy course.

  - Palliative care follow-up to be scheduled in 1-2 weeks
  - Pain management: current regimen (scheduled acetaminophen + PRN oxycodone) appears adequate; will reassess pain at each visit; consider pain management/palliative care escalation if needed
  - Nutritional decline: dietitian referral placed; consider appetite stimulant (megestrol or mirtazapine) given 6-lb unintentional weight loss — will defer to dietitian input and palliative care recommendation
  - Social work referral placed for caregiver support and advance care planning assistance
  - Home health nursing evaluation to be arranged for support between infusion visits

*Problem 4: Hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus — stable*
BP 132/80 today, adequately controlled on amlodipine 10 mg. No adjustment needed. Metformin continued for diabetes; glucose 112 mg/dL fasting, HbA1c 6.8% at last check. Note: dexamethasone (part of anti-emetic regimen) may transiently worsen glycemic control; counsel patient to monitor glucose around chemotherapy days and contact PCP if glucose >250 mg/dL.

*Problem 5: Bone health*
Denosumab (Xgeva) continued for bone protection in the setting of extensive osseous metastases. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation maintained. Dental clearance to be confirmed prior to next denosumab dose given ONJ risk (prior dental exam 9 months ago).

---

**RETURN VISIT / FOLLOW-UP**
The patient will return to the infusion suite for Cycle 1 Day 1 of carboplatin/etoposide within the next 5-7 days pending pre-chemotherapy lab confirmation. Follow-up clinic visit scheduled approximately 3 weeks thereafter (Day 22, Cycle 1 reassessment). Palliative care co-management to be initiated in parallel.

Total visit time: 75 minutes (counseling and care coordination greater than 50% of visit time)

---

Electronically signed,
Attending, Genitourinary Medical Oncology
